title: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli

items.subtopic: Diseases Caused by Gram-Negative Enteric Bacilli | GENERAL FEATURES AND PRINCIPLES | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Understanding the Urgency of Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE)
items.content: The postantibiotic era is marked by the emergence of CRE, which are designated as urgent and critical threats by global health organizations. These pathogens cause significant morbidity and mortality worldwide, particularly in low- and middle-income countries.
keyPoints: CRE are classified as an 'urgent' threat level by CDC and WHO.
CRE caused over 100,000 deaths globally in 2019.
The disease burden is higher in low- and middle-income regions like the Indian Subcontinent.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · Diseases Caused by Gram-Negative Enteric Bacilli | GENERAL FEATURES AND PRINCIPLES | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Diseases Caused by Gram-Negative Enteric Bacilli | GENERAL FEATURES AND PRINCIPLES | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Epidemiology of Enterobacterales in Healthcare Settings
items.content: Enterobacterales, including E. coli and Klebsiella, are part of the normal colonic microbiota but can become dominant colonizers in healthcare settings due to factors like antimicrobial use and prolonged hospital stays.
keyPoints: E. coli is the predominant GNB species in healthy human colonic microbiota.
Klebsiella and Enterobacter follow E. coli in prevalence within the colonic microbiota.
LTCFs are significant reservoirs for resistant GNB, increasing infection risks.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · Diseases Caused by Gram-Negative Enteric Bacilli | GENERAL FEATURES AND PRINCIPLES | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Role of Outer Membrane Components in GNB Pathogenesis and Resistance
items.content: The outer membrane of Gram-negative bacilli (GNB) is crucial for pathogenesis and antimicrobial resistance. It contains proteins, lipoproteins, and polysaccharides like capsules and lipopolysaccharide that interact with the host environment.
keyPoints: Outer membrane components are critical determinants in pathogenesis and antimicrobial resistance.
Includes capsule, lipopolysaccharide, permeability barrier, and efflux pumps.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION | PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Virulence Factors and Host Defense Mechanisms Against GNB
items.content: GNB possess specialized virulence genes that facilitate efficient infection. Host defenses include innate immunity (skin, mucosal barriers) and humoral immunity. Dysfunction in these defenses increases susceptibility to infections.
keyPoints: Specialized virulence genes define pathogens.
Host defense includes innate immunity and humoral immunity.
Dysfunction or deficiencies in host components increase infection risk.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION | PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Antigenic Variability and Challenges in Vaccine Development for GNB
items.content: GNB exhibit significant antigenic variability, with many serotypes existing within genera like E. coli. This variability complicates vaccine development by enabling immune evasion and recurrent infections.
keyPoints: E. coli has >150 O antigens, 80 K antigens, and 53 H antigens.
Antigenic variability allows immune evasion and recurrent infections.
This variability impedes vaccine development.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION | PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Mortality Rates in Severe GNB Infections
items.content: Gram-negative bacilli (GNB) can cause severe infections such as pneumonia, sepsis, and septic shock, particularly in vulnerable populations like LTCF residents and hospitalized patients. The mortality rates for these conditions are notably high.
keyPoints: Severe GNB infections include pneumonia, sepsis, and septic shock.
Mortality rates range from 20% to 60%, correlating with illness severity, host status, and antimicrobial resistance.
GNB such as E. coli and K. pneumoniae are highly virulent, even in healthy individuals.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · INFECTIOUS SYNDROMES | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Diagnosis of GNB Infections: Differentiating Colonization from Infection
items.content: Isolation of Gram-negative bacilli (GNB) from sterile sites typically indicates infection, whereas isolation from nonsterile sites requires careful clinical correlation to distinguish between colonization and true infection.
keyPoints: Isolation from sterile sites implies infection; nonsterile sites require clinical correlation.
Nonsterile site isolations include open wounds, respiratory tract, and urine with indwelling catheters.
New diagnostic methods like MALDI-TOF-MS, NAATs, and sequencing enhance pathogen identification and resistance gene detection.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · INFECTIOUS SYNDROMES | DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Principles Guiding Treatment in the Era of Increasing Antimicrobial Resistance
items.title: Early Empirical Therapy and Culturing Importance
items.content: Initiating appropriate empirical antimicrobial therapy early improves outcomes for serious GNB infections. It is critical to culture the primary site of infection before starting therapy, especially obtaining blood cultures for systemically ill patients.
keyPoints: Empirical therapy should be initiated early in serious GNB infections.
Culturing the primary site and obtaining blood cultures are essential steps before initiating antimicrobial therapy.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · Principles Guiding Treatment in the Era of Increasing Antimicrobial Resistance

items.subtopic: Principles Guiding Treatment in the Era of Increasing Antimicrobial Resistance
items.title: Antimicrobial Stewardship and De-escalation Strategy
items.content: Use two potentially active agents initially, pending susceptibility results. Once available, switch to a narrower-spectrum agent to practice responsible antimicrobial stewardship, reducing resistance selection and minimizing C. difficile infection risks.
keyPoints: Initially use two potentially active agents for serious infections.
Switch to the most appropriate narrower-spectrum agent once susceptibility results are available.
Responsible antimicrobial stewardship helps disrupt resistance cycles and reduce C. difficile infection risk.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · Principles Guiding Treatment in the Era of Increasing Antimicrobial Resistance

items.subtopic: Principles Guiding Treatment in the Era of Increasing Antimicrobial Resistance
items.title: Considerations for Antimicrobial Choice Against Enterobacterales
items.content: Select antimicrobials based on site-specific achievable drug levels and pharmacodynamic strategies. The most reliably active agents include carbapenems (except imipenem), amikacin, cefepime, and specific $\beta$-lactamase inhibitor combinations.
keyPoints: Choose antimicrobials based on site-specific achievable drug levels and pharmacodynamic strategies.
Carbapenems, amikacin, cefepime, and certain $\beta$-lactamase inhibitors are broadly active against Enterobacterales.
Imipenem is ineffective against Proteeae; tetracyclines have suboptimal concentrations at some sites.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · Principles Guiding Treatment in the Era of Increasing Antimicrobial Resistance

items.title: Understanding ESBLs in Enterobacterales
items.content: Extended-spectrum $\beta$-lactamases (ESBLs) are enzymes that confer resistance to a broad range of antibiotics, including third-generation cephalosporins and aztreonam. They often coexist with porin mutations, further reducing susceptibility. The prevalence of ESBL-producing Enterobacterales is highest in China and Eastern Europe.
keyPoints: ESBLs hydrolyze third- and fourth-generation cephalosporins, aztreonam, and more.
Porin mutations often accompany ESBL production, reducing drug uptake.
Prevalence: China > Eastern Europe > other parts of Asia > Latin America and Africa > Western Europe, the US, Canada, Australia.
Carbapenems are most reliably active against ESBL-producing strains.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · ANTIMICROBIAL TREATMENT AND RESISTANCE MECHANISMS (part 1)

items.title: Treatment Options for AmpC $\beta$-lactamase Producers
items.content: AmpC $\beta$-lactamases confer resistance to cephalosporins and cephamycins, except fourth-generation cephalosporins. They are primarily chromosomal but can be plasmid-mediated in rare cases. Carbapenems are the preferred treatment for AmpC producers.
keyPoints: AmpC enzymes resist third- to fourth-generation cephalosporins and cephamycins.
Chromosomal, with exceptions like K. pneumoniae and Proteus mirabilis.
Risk of induction is highest in Enterobacter cloacae and Klebsiella aerogenes.
Carbapenems are the treatment of choice for AmpC producers.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · ANTIMICROBIAL TREATMENT AND RESISTANCE MECHANISMS (part 1)

items.title: Oral and Carbapenem-Sparing Options for ESBL-Producing Strains
items.content: Limited oral options exist for treating ESBL-producing Enterobacterales. Fosfomycin, nitrofurantoin, pivmecillinam, and omadacycline are active against E. coli. Piperacillin-tazobactam is not recommended for bloodstream infections.
keyPoints: Oral options include fosfomycin, nitrofurantoin (75–90% susceptible), pivmecillinam, omadacycline.
Piperacillin-tazobactam not supported for bloodstream infections per MERINO trial.
Carbapenem-sparing alternatives: fluoroquinolones, TMP-SMX, aminoglycosides (if in vitro susceptible).
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · ANTIMICROBIAL TREATMENT AND RESISTANCE MECHANISMS (part 1)

items.title: First-line Treatment Options for Class A and D Carbapenemase-Producing Enterobacterales
items.content: Ceftazidime-avibactam is emerging as a first-line treatment, especially for bacteremia caused by KPC and OXA-producing CRE. However, its efficacy may be suboptimal in pneumonia cases or in patients on renal replacement therapy, with resistance developing in up to 10% of cases.
keyPoints: Ceftazidime-avibactam is a first-line agent for bacteremia due to KPC and OXA-producing CRE.
Suboptimal efficacy observed in pneumonia and patients on renal replacement therapy.
Resistance developed in up to 10% of cases.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · ANTIMICROBIAL TREATMENT AND RESISTANCE MECHANISMS (part 2)

items.title: Challenges in Treating Class B MBL-Producing CRE
items.content: Treatment is challenging due to nephrotoxicity and neurotoxicity of polymyxins, limited efficacy, and emerging resistance. Aztreonam plus avibactam shows promise with in vitro activity against class A, B, and D enzymes.
keyPoints: Polymyxins are a last line of defense but have nephrotoxicity and neurotoxicity risks.
Emerging polymyxin resistance threatens utility.
Aztreonam plus avibactam is being assessed for efficacy against MBL-producing CRE.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · ANTIMICROBIAL TREATMENT AND RESISTANCE MECHANISMS (part 2)

items.title: Resistance Mechanisms and Treatment Implications
items.content: Fluoroquinolone resistance is prevalent among GNB due to target site alterations, decreased permeability, or active efflux. It's associated with resistance to other antimicrobials, making fluoroquinolones unreliable for empirical therapy in critically ill patients.
keyPoints: 20–80% of ESBL-producing enteric GNB are also resistant to fluoroquinolones.
Fluoroquinolone resistance involves target site alterations and active efflux.
Fluoroquinolones should not be used empirically for GNB infections in critically ill patients.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · ANTIMICROBIAL TREATMENT AND RESISTANCE MECHANISMS (part 2)

items.subtopic: ANTIMICROBIAL TREATMENT AND RESISTANCE MECHANISMS (part 3) | ☐ PREVENTION | ESCHERICHIA COLI INFECTIONS
items.title: Why Amikacin is Preferred in Aminoglycoside-Resistant Enterobacterales?
items.content: Amikacin is generally more active against aminoglycoside-resistant Enterobacterales due to its reduced susceptibility to enzymatic modification by N-acetyltransferases, O-adenylyltransferases, or O-phosphotransferases. These enzymes typically affect ribosomal binding of other aminoglycosides like gentamicin and tobramycin.
keyPoints: Aminoglycoside resistance in Enterobacterales is conferred via enzymatic modification by N-acetyltransferases, O-adenylyltransferases, or O-phosphotransferases.
Amikacin is less affected by these transferases compared to gentamicin and tobramycin.
Acquired 16S ribosomal RNA methyltransferases are a rare resistance mechanism preventing all parenterally administered aminoglycosides from binding.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · ANTIMICROBIAL TREATMENT AND RESISTANCE MECHANISMS (part 3) | ☐ PREVENTION | ESCHERICHIA COLI INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: ANTIMICROBIAL TREATMENT AND RESISTANCE MECHANISMS (part 3) | ☐ PREVENTION | ESCHERICHIA COLI INFECTIONS
items.title: How to Decrease Infection Risk and Prevent Resistance in Healthcare Settings?
items.content: Implementing antimicrobial stewardship programs, adhering to hand hygiene protocols, using indwelling devices only when necessary, avoiding multiuse medication vials, and applying chlorhexidine can decrease infection risk. Universal decolonization and specific precautions for CRE are recommended.
keyPoints: Antimicrobial stewardship programs minimize resistance development by facilitating appropriate antimicrobial use.
Hand hygiene and cleaning/disinfection of patient-contact objects are essential.
Indwelling devices should be used according to protocols with daily-use evaluation.
Chlorhexidine application reduces pneumonia incidence in ventilated patients.
Universal decolonization (e.g., chlorhexidine bathing) is supported for ICU or nursing home residents.
Contact precautions and environmental cleaning are recommended for CRE.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · ANTIMICROBIAL TREATMENT AND RESISTANCE MECHANISMS (part 3) | ☐ PREVENTION | ESCHERICHIA COLI INFECTIONS

items.title: Understanding Commensal E. coli and Risk Factors for Extraintestinal Infections
items.content: Commensal E. coli strains are part of the normal intestinal microbiota, providing benefits such as resistance to pathogenic colonization. Despite lacking virulence traits typical of pathogenic strains, they can cause extraintestinal infections under certain conditions.
keyPoints: Commensal E. coli generally lack specialized virulence traits.

Can cause extraintestinal infections with aggravating factors like foreign bodies (e.g., urinary catheter), host compromise (e.g., anatomical or functional abnormalities, systemic immunocompromise), or large inoculum/mixed bacterial species.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · COMMENSAL STRAINS

items.title: Factors Leading to Extraintestinal Infections by Commensal E. coli
items.content: While commensal E. coli strains are typically non-pathogenic, they can become pathogenic in the presence of certain risk factors such as foreign bodies or host immune compromise.
keyPoints: Aggravating factors include foreign bodies (e.g., urinary catheter), host compromise (e.g., anatomic/functional abnormalities like obstruction, systemic immunocompromise), and large inoculum/mixed species.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · COMMENSAL STRAINS

items.title: Understanding ExPEC Infections: Mechanisms and Challenges
items.content: ExPEC strains are significant contributors to both community-acquired and healthcare-associated infections. Their ability to acquire antimicrobial resistance mechanisms, such as fluoroquinolone resistance mutations, ESBLs, and carbapenemases, complicates treatment strategies. The global spread of certain clonal groups like ST131 highlights the importance of understanding transmission dynamics.
keyPoints: ExPEC strains are common causes of extraintestinal infections.
They acquire antimicrobial resistance mechanisms (e.g., FQ resistance mutations, ESBLs, carbapenemases).
ST131 is a globally disseminated clonal group with efficient human-to-human transmission.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · EXTRAINTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 1)

items.title: UTI Prevalence and Impact of E. coli
items.content: Urinary tract infections are the most frequent extraintestinal infections caused by ExPEC, particularly E. coli. In the U.S., E. coli is responsible for 80-90% of cystitis cases in ambulatory premenopausal women with uncomplicated urinary tracts. Recurrence rates and potential complications like pyelonephritis highlight the need for tailored treatment approaches.
keyPoints: UTIs are second only to lower respiratory tract infections in hospitalization causes.
E. coli causes 80-90% of cystitis episodes in premenopausal women with uncomplicated UTIs annually.
20% of women experience frequent recurrences of cystitis.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · EXTRAINTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 1)

items.title: Abdominal and Pelvic Infections by E. coli
items.content: E. coli is a leading pathogen in abdominal and pelvic infections, often found alongside other microbiota members. Clinical syndromes include peritonitis, diverticulitis, appendicitis, and septic cholangitis/cholecystitis. Understanding the diverse presentations and potential for mixed infections is crucial for effective management.
keyPoints: Abdominal/pelvic infections are a common site for E. coli extraintestinal infections.
Clinical syndromes include peritonitis, diverticulitis, appendicitis, and septic cholangitis/cholecystitis.
E. coli can be isolated alone or with other intestinal microbiota members.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · EXTRAINTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 1)

items.title: E. coli Pneumonia: A Serious Consideration in Specific Populations
items.content: Pneumonia caused by E. coli, while not a common cause of community-acquired pneumonia, becomes more prevalent among residents of long-term care facilities and patients with hospital-acquired pneumonia, particularly postoperative and ICU patients.
keyPoints: Enteric GNB account for 1–3% of cases of community-acquired pneumonia.
E. coli is the third or fourth most commonly isolated type of GNB in hospital-acquired pneumonia, accounting for 5–8%.
Pneumonia due to ExPEC has high crude and attributable mortality rates (20–60% and 10–20%, respectively).
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · EXTRAINTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 2)

items.title: E. coli Meningitis: Predominantly a Neonatal Concern
items.content: Neonatal meningitis is frequently caused by E. coli, especially strains with the K1 capsular antigen from specific clonal groups.
keyPoints: E. coli is one of the leading causes of neonatal meningitis alongside group B Streptococcus.
Most E. coli strains causing neonatal meningitis possess the K1 capsular antigen and derive from ST95, ST59, ST62 clonal groups.
After the first month of life, E. coli meningitis is uncommon except in cases involving surgical or traumatic disruption or hepatic cirrhosis.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · EXTRAINTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 2)

items.title: E. coli Bacteremia: Common Source and Clinical Significance
items.content: E. coli bacteremia often arises from urinary tract infections, particularly in patients with pyelonephritis or urinary obstruction.
keyPoints: The urinary tract is the most common source of E. coli bacteremia, accounting for one-half to two-thirds of episodes.
Bacteremia can also arise from abdominal sources, soft tissue, bone, pulmonary infections, and intravascular catheter infections.
Isolation of E. coli from blood is clinically significant and may be accompanied by sepsis syndrome or septic shock.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · EXTRAINTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 2)

items.subtopic: EXTRAINTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 3) | Extraintestinal E. coli Infections
items.title: Understanding Antimicrobial Resistance in ExPEC Strains
items.content: Extraintestinal pathogenic Escherichia coli (ExPEC) strains exhibit significant antimicrobial resistance, impacting treatment choices. More than 90% of these strains are rapid lactose fermenters and indole-positive, aiding identification. Increasingly acquired resistance to common antibiotics like ampicillin and fluoroquinolones (>20% prevalence) necessitates alternative treatments for serious infections.
keyPoints: ExPEC strains grow aerobically and anaerobically within 24 hours on standard media.
>90% of ExPEC strains are rapid lactose fermenters and indole-positive.
Acquired resistance in ExPEC makes treatment challenging, with >20% resistance to ampicillin, amoxicillin-clavulanate, cefazolin, TMP-SMX, and fluoroquinolones.
>90% of uncomplicated cystitis-causing isolates remain susceptible to nitrofurantoin and fosfomycin.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · EXTRAINTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 3) | Extraintestinal E. coli Infections

items.subtopic: EXTRAINTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 3) | Extraintestinal E. coli Infections
items.title: Treatment Strategies for ESBL-Producing E. coli Strains
items.content: ESBL-producing E. coli strains, identified in 24% of U.S. clinical isolates from 2015 to 2017, pose treatment challenges due to limited oral options. Fosfomycin, pivmecillinam, and nitrofurantoin are effective for cystitis but not pyelonephritis. For carbapenem-susceptible strains, agents like carbapenems and ceftazidime-avibactam show >90% activity, though efficacy is site-specific.
keyPoints: 24% of U.S. E. coli clinical isolates were ESBL-producers from 2015 to 2017.
Fosfomycin, pivmecillinam, and nitrofurantoin are effective for cystitis caused by ESBL-producers.
>90% activity in carbapenem-susceptible strains with agents like carbapenems, ceftazidime-avibactam, and piperacillin-tazobactam.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · EXTRAINTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 3) | Extraintestinal E. coli Infections

items.title: Understanding the Pathogenic Mechanisms of STEC/EHEC/ST-EAEC
items.content: Shiga toxin-producing E. coli strains, including STEC, EHEC, and ST-EAEC, are significant pathogens causing hemorrhagic colitis and hemolytic-uremic syndrome (HUS). These strains are more prevalent in high-income countries due to their transmission through contaminated food and water sources such as undercooked ground beef and fresh produce. The critical virulence factor is the production of Shiga toxin, particularly Stx2a-g and/or Stx1a,c,d, which inhibits ribosomal function leading to apoptosis.
keyPoints: STEC/EHEC/ST-EAEC causes hemorrhagic colitis and HUS.
Transmission occurs primarily through contaminated food and water in high-income countries.
Shiga toxin production (Stx2a-g and/or Stx1a,c,d) is critical for disease manifestation.
< $10^{2}$ CFU can cause disease, highlighting the pathogen's potency.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · INTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 1)

items.title: Transmission and Epidemiology of Intestinal Pathogenic E. coli
items.content: Intestinal pathogenic E. coli strains, including STEC/EHEC/ST-EAEC, are transmitted primarily through contaminated food and water in high-income countries, while person-to-person transmission is more common for EPEC. These pathogens exploit species-specific attachment factors to infect hosts, with humans being the major reservoir except for STEC/EHEC, which predominantly involves bovines.
keyPoints: STEC/EHEC/ST-EAEC primarily transmitted via contaminated food and water in high-income countries.
EPEC spreads mainly through person-to-person contact.
Humans are the main reservoir for these strains except STEC/EHEC, which involves bovines.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · INTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Impact and Outbreaks of Shiga Toxin-Producing E. coli
items.content: STEC strains are a leading cause of bacterial diarrhea in the United States, with O157:H7 being the most prominent serotype. Notable outbreaks have occurred due to contaminated produce and undercooked beef, emphasizing the importance of food safety measures. The 2011 outbreak involving an EAEC strain acquiring Shiga toxin highlights the potential for novel genotypes to cause significant morbidity.
keyPoints: STEC is the fourth most common cause of bacterial diarrhea in the U.S.
O157:H7 is a prominent serotype among STEC strains.
Outbreaks often involve contaminated produce and undercooked beef.
The 2011 outbreak involved an EAEC strain acquiring Shiga toxin, leading to >4000 cases.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · INTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 1)

items.title: Understanding EHEC Pathogenicity
items.content: EHEC strains, a subgroup of STEC, require the presence of stx1 and/or stx2 genes along with LEE for full pathogenicity. These components facilitate adherence to intestinal epithelial cells and subvert host cell functions via type III secretion system.
keyPoints: LEE is essential for EHEC pathogenicity.
EHEC strains possess stx1 and/or stx2 genes in addition to LEE.
Type III secretion system mediates bacterial protein translocation.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · INTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 2)

items.title: HUS Complications from STEC/EHEC Infections
items.content: A significant complication of STEC/EHEC infection is Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (HUS), which manifests 2–14 days post-diarrhea, predominantly in young children and elderly patients. HUS results from systemic translocation of Shiga toxins leading to thrombotic microangiopathy.
keyPoints: HUS occurs 2–14 days after diarrhea onset.
15% incidence in infected children <10 years old.
Over 50% of U.S. HUS cases are STEC/EHEC-related.
Characteristics: fever, hemolytic anemia (hematocrit <30%), thrombocytopenia (<150,000/mm³), renal failure, encephalopathy.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · INTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 2)

items.title: Diagnosis and Presentation of ETEC Infections
items.content: ETEC infections cause traveler's diarrhea with an incubation period of 12–72 hours. Disease results from adherence to enterocytes via colonization factors, leading to fluid secretion by LT or ST toxins.
keyPoints: ETEC is a major cause of traveler's diarrhea (25–75% cases).
Incubation period: 12–72 hours.
Large inoculum ($10^6 - 10^8$ CFU) needed for disease.
LT and ST toxins lead to watery diarrhea without histopathologic changes in the small bowel.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · INTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 2)

items.title: ETEC Infection: Recognizing Severity and Mortality
items.content: ETEC infection can range from mild to life-threatening, with significant morbidity and mortality primarily due to profound volume depletion. This is especially critical in settings with limited healthcare access or suitable rehydration fluids.
keyPoints: Symptoms usually last 3–5 days but may result in >250,000 deaths annually.
High risk when affecting small/undernourished children.
Profound volume depletion is a major cause of mortality.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · INTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 3)

items.title: EPEC Infection: Risk Factors and Pathogenesis
items.content: EPEC primarily affects young children, causing diarrhea often accompanied by vomiting and fever. Breastfeeding reduces incidence, while rapid person-to-person spread is possible.
keyPoints: First recognized E. coli pathotype for diarrheal disease in the 1940s-1950s.
Breastfeeding diminishes incidence.
Diarrhea lasts 5–15 days; may persist longer.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · INTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 3)

items.title: EIEC Infection: Clinical Features and Virulence
items.content: EIEC, similar to Shigella, causes mild, self-limited diarrhea with a large inoculum requirement. It can occasionally cause shigellosis-like syndrome.
keyPoints: Rarely identified in the U.S., more common in low- and middle-income countries.
Large inoculum ($10^8 - 10^{10}$ CFU) required for disease.
Typically causes mild, watery diarrhea lasting 7–10 days.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · INTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 3)

items.subtopic: INTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 4) | Intestinal E. coli Infections
items.title: Why Test All Cases of Community-Acquired Diarrhea for STEC/EHEC/ST-EAEC?
items.content: The CDC recommends evaluating all patients with community-acquired diarrhea, regardless of clinical features, to detect STEC/EHEC/ST-EAEC infections. This is crucial because bloody stool and fecal white blood cells are not reliable indicators of these infections. Using both culture and toxin detection increases diagnostic sensitivity.
keyPoints: CDC recommends testing all cases of community-acquired diarrhea for STEC/EHEC/ST-EAEC.
Bloody stool and fecal white blood cells are not reliable indicators.
Simultaneous culture and Shiga toxin detection increase diagnostic sensitivity.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · INTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 4) | Intestinal E. coli Infections

items.subtopic: INTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 4) | Intestinal E. coli Infections
items.title: How to Manage STEC/EHEC/ST-EAEC Infection?
items.content: The primary treatment for diarrheal syndromes, including STEC/EHEC/ST-EAEC infections, is the replacement of water and electrolytes to prevent renal injury. Prophylactic antibiotics should be avoided due to high resistance rates. For traveler's diarrhea without severe symptoms, fluoroquinolones or azithromycin can reduce illness duration, while loperamide may quickly alleviate symptoms.
keyPoints: Mainstay treatment is water and electrolyte replacement.
Prophylactic antibiotics generally discouraged due to resistance.
Fluoroquinolones or azithromycin can shorten traveler's diarrhea duration.
Antimicrobial therapy for STEC/EHEC/ST-EAEC should be avoided to prevent HUS.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · INTESTINAL PATHOGENIC STRAINS (part 4) | Intestinal E. coli Infections

items.title: Understanding the Epidemiology and Transmission of K. pneumoniae
items.content: Klebsiella pneumoniae is a significant pathogen in both community-acquired and healthcare-associated infections, with classic strains (cKp) being prevalent in hospitals and long-term care facilities. These strains are notorious for acquiring antimicrobial resistance determinants, contributing to their spread within and between institutions.
keyPoints: K. pneumoniae colonizes 5–35% of the human colon and 1–5% of the oropharynx.
cKp is nearly fourfold more transmissible than E. coli.
Carbapenemase-producing strains are associated with increased spread compared to carbapenem-susceptible strains.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · KLEBSIELLA INFECTIONS

items.title: Characteristics and Impact of Hypervirulent K. pneumoniae (hvKp)
items.content: Hypervirulent K. pneumoniae strains are distinct from classic cKp, causing severe infections in younger, healthy individuals, often with diabetes mellitus. These strains can lead to metastatic spread and multiple infection sites, raising concerns about their increasing role in healthcare-associated infections.
keyPoints: hvKp was first recognized in Taiwan in 1986.
Infections occur globally but are more common among Asians from the Asian Pacific Rim.
hvKp can cause serious infections like liver abscesses, meningitis, and necrotizing fasciitis.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · KLEBSIELLA INFECTIONS

items.title: Emerging Threats: MDR and XDR hvKp
items.content: The convergence of antimicrobial resistance determinants from cKp with virulence factors from hvKp has led to the emergence of multidrug-resistant (MDR) and extensively drug-resistant (XDR) hvKp strains, posing significant challenges in treatment and management.
keyPoints: Confluence of carbapenemase/ESBL genes with hvKp virulence factors.
Evolution of MDR and XDR hvKp strains.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · KLEBSIELLA INFECTIONS

items.title: Klebsiella Pneumonia: Recognizing Risk Factors and Presentation Patterns
items.content: Klebsiella pneumonia, particularly due to hvKp, is increasingly common in certain populations. It's important to recognize the risk factors such as oropharyngeal colonization in LTCF residents and hospitalized patients, mechanical ventilation, and its rising incidence in Asia and South Africa. Presentation patterns have shifted from classic lobar infiltrates to earlier, less extensive infections.
keyPoints: cKp is a common cause of pneumonia among LTCF residents and hospitalized patients due to oropharyngeal colonization.
Mechanical ventilation significantly increases the risk for Klebsiella pneumonia.
In Asia and South Africa, hvKp rivals S. pneumoniae in causing community-acquired pneumonia, even in younger patients without underlying disease.
Presentation has shifted from classic lobar infiltrates to earlier, less extensive infections.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · INFECTIOUS SYNDROMES

items.title: Klebsiella as a Cause of Abdominal Infections: Pyogenic Liver Abscess and Beyond
items.content: hvKp is increasingly recognized as a major cause of monomicrobial community-acquired pyogenic liver abscess, particularly in the Asian Pacific Rim. It has surpassed E. coli in frequency for this condition over recent decades. hvKp also contributes to spontaneous bacterial peritonitis and splenic abscess.
keyPoints: hvKp is a common cause of monomicrobial community-acquired pyogenic liver abscess, especially in the Asian Pacific Rim.
Over the past two decades, hvKp has replaced E. coli as the most common pathogen for this syndrome in the region.
hvKp is increasingly implicated in spontaneous bacterial peritonitis and splenic abscess.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · INFECTIOUS SYNDROMES

items.title: Identifying hvKp: Biomarkers vs. String Test
items.content: While the hypermucoviscous phenotype of hvKp can be assessed using the string test, its sensitivity and specificity are suboptimal. The most accurate method for identifying hvKp involves detecting all five biomarkers (iucA, iroB, peg-344, rmpA, and rmpA2), although this comprehensive testing is not routinely available.
keyPoints: Hypermucoviscous phenotype of hvKp can be assessed with the string test; however, sensitivity and specificity are less than optimal.
Identification of all 5 biomarkers (iucA, iroB, peg-344, rmpA, rmpA2) is most accurate for identifying hvKp in both antimicrobial sensitive and MDR isolates.
Comprehensive testing for these biomarkers is not routinely available.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · DIAGNOSIS | Klebsiella Infections

items.title: Prevalence and Resistance Patterns of K. pneumoniae
items.content: Klebsiella pneumoniae exhibits significant antibiotic resistance, with over 30% prevalence for resistance to amoxicillin-clavulanate, ampicillin-sulbactam, nitrofurantoin, and TMP-SMX. Approximately 10-20% of isolates are resistant to fluoroquinolones, piperacillin-tazobactam, fosfomycin, and omadacycline. ESBL-producing strains constitute about 25% according to USNHSN data from 2015–2017, with higher rates in Asia, South America, and Africa.
keyPoints: K. pneumoniae is intrinsically resistant to ampicillin; resistance >30% for amoxicillin-clavulanate, ampicillin-sulbactam, nitrofurantoin, TMP-SMX.
~10–20% resistance in K. pneumoniae for fluoroquinolones, piperacillin-tazobactam, fosfomycin, omadacycline.
25% of K. pneumoniae were ESBL-producing strains (USNHSN 2015-2017); higher rates reported from Asia, South America, Africa.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · DIAGNOSIS | Klebsiella Infections

items.title: Proteus mirabilis: A Leading Cause of Complicated UTIs and Catheter-Associated Infections
items.content: P. mirabilis is the predominant species causing Proteus infections, especially in complicated urinary tract infections (UTIs) associated with catheterization. Its ability to produce urease leads to urine alkalization, promoting struvite stone formation and biofilm development on catheters.
keyPoints: P. mirabilis causes 90% of Proteus infections.
Proteus species account for 10-15% of complicated UTIs, primarily those associated with catheterization.
Proteus is responsible for 20-45% of urine isolates from chronically catheterized patients.
Urease production by Proteus leads to alkaline urine and struvite stone formation.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · PROTEUS INFECTIONS | INFECTIOUS SYNDROMES

items.title: Proteus Infections Beyond the Urinary Tract: Recognizing Rare but Serious Complications
items.content: While Proteus primarily causes urinary tract infections, it can also lead to other serious infections such as pneumonia, intraabdominal abscesses, and bacteremia. These infections are more common in long-term care facility residents or hospitalized patients.
keyPoints: Proteus occasionally causes pneumonia, especially in LTCF residents or hospitalized patients.
Intraabdominal abscesses, biliary tract infection, and surgical site infections can occur.
Proteus is a rare cause of neonatal meningitis, often complicated by cerebral abscess formation.
Most episodes of Proteus bacteremia originate from the urinary tract.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · PROTEUS INFECTIONS | INFECTIOUS SYNDROMES

items.title: Key Laboratory Features and Resistance Patterns in Proteus Infections
items.content: Proteus infections are characterized by specific laboratory features such as lactose-negative growth, H2S production, swarming motility, and distinct odor. Differentiation among species is based on indole production and ornithine decarboxylase activity. Resistance patterns include intrinsic resistance to several antibiotics and acquired resistance to others.
keyPoints: Proteus spp. are lactose-negative, produce H2S, show swarming motility, and have a distinct odor.
P. mirabilis and P. penneri are indole-negative; P. vulgaris is indole-positive.
Ornithine decarboxylase activity differentiates P. penneri from P. mirabilis.
Intrinsic resistance in Proteus spp. to nitrofurantoin, polymyxins, imipenem, and tetracycline derivatives.
Acquired resistance rates: ampicillin (15–65%) in P. mirabilis; fluoroquinolones (10–55%), fosfomycin (7–22%), TMP-SMX (20–50%) in Proteus spp.
Ampicillin-sulbactam resistance due to ESBLs: 5–10% in the U.S., up to 60% in Asia.
Carbapenem resistance <10%, but MBL production limits treatment options.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · DIAGNOSIS | Proteus Infections | ENTEROBACTER AND CRONOBACTER INFECTIONS

items.title: Epidemiology and Resistance of Enterobacter Infections
items.content: Enterobacter infections are primarily health care-related, with E. cloacae complex being the most common cause. Cronobacter spp. are less frequently isolated. Colonization increases in healthcare settings, and extensive antibiotic resistance contributes to their nosocomial prevalence.
keyPoints: E. cloacae complex is responsible for most Enterobacter infections.
Cronobacter sakazakii and other species are rarely isolated (<1%).
Enterobacter spp. colonization increases with LTCF residence or hospitalization.
Risk factors: prior antibiotic treatment, comorbid disease, ICU residency.
Extensive antibiotic resistance in Enterobacter spp. contributes to nosocomial infections.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · DIAGNOSIS | Proteus Infections | ENTEROBACTER AND CRONOBACTER INFECTIONS

items.title: Enterobacter Infection Sources and Neonatal Risks
items.content: Enterobacter infections can arise from various sources, including contaminated medical devices and fluids. Notably, neonates, especially those with low birth weight, are at risk for severe infections like C. sakazakii bacteremia, necrotizing enterocolitis, and meningitis, often linked to powdered infant formula contamination.
keyPoints: Common syndromes: pneumonia, UTI (catheter-associated), intravascular device-related infection, surgical site infection, abdominal infection.
Neonatal risks include C. sakazakii infections leading to bacteremia, necrotizing enterocolitis, and meningitis.
WHO recommends reconstituting powdered infant formula with hot water (>70°C) and storing it at <5°C or minimizing storage time.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · INFECTIOUS SYNDROMES | DIAGNOSIS | Enterobacter Infections

items.title: Antimicrobial Resistance in Enterobacter Infections
items.content: Enterobacter species exhibit intrinsic resistance to several antibiotics, with varying prevalence of acquired resistance. Cefepime is a viable treatment option unless ESBLs are present. Carbapenem resistance remains uncommon but is increasing due to porin mutations and carbapenemase acquisition.
keyPoints: Intrinsic resistance: ampicillin, first-generation cephalosporins, cephamycins.
Acquired resistance prevalence: 15-40% for piperacillin-tazobactam; 5-23% for polymyxins; 15-17% for fosfomycin; 15-30% for TMP-SMX; 5-20% for fluoroquinolones.
Cefepime is stable to AmpC $ \beta $-lactamases but not suitable if ESBLs are co-produced.
Carbapenem resistance prevalence: ~5%, often due to porin mutations and increased AmpC expression.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · INFECTIOUS SYNDROMES | DIAGNOSIS | Enterobacter Infections

items.title: Prevention of Serratia Infections in Healthcare Settings
items.content: Serratia infections are primarily health care-associated, often due to contamination from medical equipment or improper hygiene. Preventive measures include maintaining high standards of hand hygiene, sterile techniques, and strict infection control protocols.
keyPoints: S. marcescens is responsible for >90% of Serratia infections.
Reservoirs in healthcare settings include hands, medical devices, IV solutions, and disinfectants like chlorhexidine.
Infections often result from direct inoculation or colonization, particularly in LTCFs and hospitals.
Outbreaks frequently involve MDR strains, especially in ICUs.
Serratia accounts for 1–3% of hospital-acquired infections.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · SERRATIA INFECTIONS | INFECTIOUS SYNDROMES

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Common Sites of Serratia Infections
items.content: Serratia primarily causes health care-associated infections, with common sites including the respiratory and genitourinary tracts, intravascular devices, and surgical wounds. It is less commonly associated with neonatal meningitis or endocarditis.
keyPoints: Primary infection sites: respiratory tract, genitourinary tract, intravascular devices, eye (keratitis), surgical wounds, bloodstream.
Most Serratia bacteremia arises from focal infections rather than contaminated infusions.
Less common syndromes include soft tissue infections, osteomyelitis, abdominal/biliary tract infections, and septic arthritis.
Rare in neonatal meningitis, postsurgical meningitis, endophthalmitis, breast implant infection, and bacteremia in neutropenic patients.
Endocarditis is rare but occurs mainly in IV drug users.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · SERRATIA INFECTIONS | INFECTIOUS SYNDROMES

items.title: Antibiotic Resistance Patterns of Serratia Species
items.content: Serratia species exhibit intrinsic resistance to several antibiotics, including ampicillin and first- and second-generation cephalosporins. However, they remain susceptible to fluoroquinolones, TMP-SMX, piperacillin-tazobactam, fosfomycin, and omadacycline in 85–95% of cases.
keyPoints: Serratia species are intrinsically resistant to ampicillin, amoxicillin-clavulanate, ampicillin-sulbactam, first- and second-generation cephalosporins, cephamycins, nitrofurantoin, and polymyxins.
>80% of Serratia strains show resistance to these antibiotics.
Fluoroquinolones, TMP-SMX, piperacillin-tazobactam, fosfomycin, and omadacycline are active against 85–95% of U.S. and European isolates.
<10% prevalence of ESBL-producing isolates in the US and globally; rates can be 20-30% in Asia and Latin America.
Resistance to aztreonam and third-generation cephalosporins generally ranges from 10 to 20%.
Carbapenemase acquisition is increasing but remains uncommon.
For critically ill patients, carbapenems, piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, amikacin, ceftazidime-avibactam, and ceftolozane-tazobactam are most active (>90% susceptibility).
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · DIAGNOSIS | Serratia Infections | CITROBACTER INFECTIONS | INFECTIOUS SYNDROMES

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Epidemiology of Citrobacter Infections
items.content: Citrobacter infections, primarily caused by C. freundii and C. koseri, are similar to Enterobacter infections and often occur in immunocompromised patients or those with disrupted barriers. They account for 1–2% of nosocomial infections.
keyPoints: C. freundii and Citrobacter koseri cause most human Citrobacter infections.
These infections are epidemiologically similar to Enterobacter infections.
Citrobacter species are present in water, food, soil, and certain animals.
Colonization is uncommon among healthy humans but increases with LTCF residence or hospitalization.
They account for 1–2% of nosocomial infections.
Affected hosts often have immunosuppression, comorbid diseases, or disrupted barriers.
Urinary tract accounts for 40–50% of Citrobacter infections.
C. koseri causes 1–2% of neonatal meningitis cases, with 50–80% complicated by brain abscess.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · DIAGNOSIS | Serratia Infections | CITROBACTER INFECTIONS | INFECTIOUS SYNDROMES

items.title: Antibiotic Resistance Patterns in Citrobacter Species
items.content: Citrobacter species exhibit varying levels of antibiotic resistance, with C. freundii showing more resistance than C. koseri. Understanding these patterns is crucial for selecting effective treatments.
keyPoints: C. freundii is intrinsically resistant to ampicillin, ampicillin-sulbactam, amoxicillin-clavulanate, first-generation cephalosporins, and cephamycins.
C. koseri exhibits intrinsic resistance to ampicillin and ampicillin-sulbactam.

Acquired resistance prevalence: 15-35% for third-generation cephalosporins, piperacillin-tazobactam, fluoroquinolones, TMP-SMX; ~10% for nitrofurantoin and omadacycline (39% if tetracycline-resistant).
ESBL production prevalence: 5-30%.
Carbapenemase acquisition in <10% of isolates.

Effective agents against Citrobacter isolates (>90% susceptible): carbapenems, amikacin, fosfomycin, polymyxins, cefepime, ceftolozane-tazobactam, ceftazidime-avibactam, cefiderocol, tigecycline, eravacycline, and omadacycline (if tetracycline-susceptible).

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Resistance of Morganella and Providencia Infections
items.content: Morganella and Providencia species are primarily urinary tract pathogens, with resistance patterns that complicate treatment in settings with extensive use of certain antibiotics.
keyPoints: Commonly cause UTIs associated with long-term catheterization (>30 days).
Infections can lead to biofilm formation, catheter encrustation, struvite stones, and renal obstruction.
Purple urine syndrome can occur due to these organisms.
Morganella is also linked to snakebite infections.
Other syndromes include surgical site infection, soft tissue infection, pneumonia (ventilator-associated), intravascular device infection, and intraabdominal infection.
Bacteremia is uncommon; urinary tract is the most common source when it occurs.
Intrinsic resistance to polymyxins and tigecycline may increase their prevalence in settings with extensive use of these agents.
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · DIAGNOSIS | Citrobacter Infections | MORGANELLA AND PROVIDENCIA INFECTIONS | INFECTIOUS SYNDROMES

items.title: Antibiotic Resistance Patterns in Morganella and Providencia Infections
items.content: Morganella morganii and Providencia species exhibit intrinsic resistance to several antibiotics, including ampicillin, first-generation cephalosporins, nitrofurantoin, tetracyclines, imipenem, and polymyxins. P. stuartii and M. morganii show significant resistance (>50%) to gentamicin and tobramycin. Resistance prevalence for third-generation cephalosporins ranges from 10-30%, fluoroquinolones from 10-40%, and TMP-SMX from 20-40%. The most effective treatments are carbapenems (except imipenem), amikacin, cefepime, ceftazidime-avibactam, ceftolozane-tazobactam, and cefiderocol.
keyPoints: Intrinsic resistance to ampicillin, first-generation cephalosporins, nitrofurantoin, tetracyclines, imipenem, polymyxins
>50% resistance of M. morganii and P. stuartii to gentamicin and tobramycin
Resistance prevalence: 10-30% for third-generation cephalosporins, 10-40% for fluoroquinolones, 20-40% for TMP-SMX
<10% ESBL production, <10% acquired carbapenemase production
>90% susceptibility to carbapenems (except imipenem), amikacin, cefepime, ceftazidime-avibactam, ceftolozane-tazobactam, and cefiderocol
items.reference: Diseases Caused by Gram Negative Enteric Bacilli · DIAGNOSIS | Morganella and Providencia Infections

items.title: Importance of Removing Colonized Devices in UTI Eradication
items.content: For effective eradication of urinary tract infections (UTIs) caused by Morganella morganii or Providencia species, it is crucial to remove any colonized urinary catheters or stones. This step is essential due to the bacteria's ability to persist on these surfaces despite antibiotic treatment.
keyPoints: Removal of colonized urinary catheter or stone critical for UTI eradication
